g.data-narration: Data structures are specialized methods of organizing and storing data in a computer system for efficient access and modification.
g.text: A specialized way of organizing and storing data in a computer to enable
efficient access and modification. They form the backbone of algorithm design.

g.data-narration: Data structures are essential for every computer science student as they directly impact application performance and efficiency.
g.text: • Foundation of algorithm design and analysis

g.data-narration: Data structures are classified into primitive (basic types) and non-primitive (complex derived types).
Primitive data types are basic types provided by programming languages: integer, float, character, and boolean.
g.text: • Linear: Arrays, Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues

g.data-narration: Linear structures arrange elements sequentially, while non-linear structures have hierarchical or multiple connections between elements.
An ADT defines a data structure by its behavior, hiding implementation details while exposing only the interface.
g.text: An ADT is a mathematical specification that defines:

g.data-narration: Common ADTs include List, Stack (LIFO), and Queue (FIFO), each with specific operation behaviors.
Time complexity is measured using Big-O notation, showing how execution time grows with input size.
Array operations have different time complexities: O(1) for access, O(n) for search, and O(n) for insertion/deletion.
g.text: Direct access via memory address calculation. Worst-case search requires checking all elements.

g.data-narration: A stack follows LIFO principle with O(1) push and pop operations, used in function call management.
Six basic operations are performed on data structures: traversal, insertion, deletion, searching, sorting, and merging.
Data structures have practical applications: stacks for function calls, queues for scheduling, trees for file systems.
Key takeaways: Data structures are fundamental, choose based on operations needed, consider time complexity, and use ADTs for abstraction.
g.text: Array access is O(1), search is O(n)

g.data-narration: Remember: classify data structures correctly, know Big-O order, understand ADT vs data structure difference, and practice tracing operations.
g.text: ✓ Know classification: primitive vs non-primitive, linear vs non-linear
✓ Array access O(1), search O(n) - don't confuse!
✓ ADT is specification (what), data structure is implementation (how)
✗ Don't mix up Stack and Queue operations
